description: RAG quality benchmark — 45 questions. 30 answerable across 7 categories (A–G) + 15 adversarial in category H (5 never-discussed, 5 anti-Peat framings, 5 subtle misattributions). Validates abstention + premise-rejection in addition to answer quality.
adversarial_baseline_note: Before Phase 2 abstention gates land, adversarial abstention/rejection rate is expected near 0%. Track improvement over phases.

questions.question: What does Ray Peat say about thyroid function and its role in metabolism?
Why does Peat consider polyunsaturated fats (PUFAs) harmful?
How does Peat view the role of CO2 in cellular health?
What is Peat's view on sugar and fructose?
Why does Peat favor saturated fats over polyunsaturated fats?
What role does progesterone play according to Peat?
How does Peat describe the relationship between estrogen and cortisol?
What does Peat say about serotonin — is it really the 'happy hormone'?
How does Peat recommend supporting thyroid function through diet?
What is Peat's stance on HRT and bioidentical hormones?
How does Peat link stress hormones to aging?
What does Peat say about milk and dairy consumption?
Why does Peat recommend orange juice?
What is Peat's view on coconut oil?
How does Peat view cruciferous vegetables like broccoli and kale?
What does Peat say about gelatin and amino acid balance?
What does Peat say about diabetes and blood sugar regulation?
How does Peat explain the causes of cancer at the metabolic level?
What is Peat's view on Alzheimer's and neurodegeneration?
How does Peat approach hypothyroidism diagnosis — are standard lab tests reliable?
What does Peat say about estrogen's role in breast cancer?
Diabetes
Stress
Aging
When does Peat say polyunsaturated fats might actually be acceptable or unavoidable?
Are there cases where Peat recommends against supplementation?
What does Peat say about the limits or uncertainties of his own recommendations?
How does Peat connect the thyroid, liver, and gut in metabolic health?
What is the relationship Peat draws between PUFAs, estrogen, and aging?
How does Peat tie CO2, lactic acid, and cellular respiration together?
What did Ray Peat say about GLP-1 agonists like Ozempic or semaglutide for weight loss and metabolic health?
questions.adversarial_note: Ozempic/semaglutide became mainstream metabolic intervention post-2022. Peat died October 2022. No corpus coverage.

questions.question: What did Peat recommend regarding continuous glucose monitors (CGMs) for metabolic optimization?
questions.adversarial_note: CGM biohacking (Levels Health, Nutrisense) is a 2020+ trend. Peat cared about metabolism but never engaged with CGM devices specifically.

questions.question: What did Peat say about NAD+ precursors like nicotinamide riboside (NR) and nicotinamide mononucleotide (NMN) for longevity?
questions.adversarial_note: NR/NMN longevity-supplementation became mainstream 2019+ (David Sinclair). Peat discussed NAD/NADH metabolism generally but not these specific commercial precursors.

questions.question: What is Peat's protocol for zone 2 cardio heart-rate training?
questions.adversarial_note: Zone 2 training as a specific protocol (Attia / Inigo San Millan popularization) is 2022+. Peat's general view on exercise-as-stress exists but he didn't prescribe zone 2.

questions.question: What did Peat say about the metabolic effects of mRNA COVID vaccines?
questions.adversarial_note: mRNA vaccine rollout 2020-2022. Peat's final years. No known direct published position; absence of corpus coverage should trigger abstention, not speculation.

questions.question: What foods does Peat recommend on a strict ketogenic or low-carb diet?
questions.adversarial_note: Peat actively opposed keto/low-carb. He considered fructose and sugar foundational for thyroid function and stress-hormone reduction. Correct response: reject the premise, explain his pro-sugar stance.

questions.question: Which seed oils does Peat recommend for cooking — is sunflower or safflower better?
questions.adversarial_note: Peat opposed all seed oils. PUFAs are one of his primary antagonists. Correct response: reject premise, recommend saturated fats (coconut, butter, beef tallow).

questions.question: Why did Peat endorse soy protein isolate as a high-quality protein for athletes?
questions.adversarial_note: Peat strongly opposed soy products. Isoflavones are estrogenic, soy oil is PUFA-heavy, and soy suppresses thyroid. Premise is false.

questions.question: What daily dose of fish oil does Peat recommend for omega-3 benefits?
questions.adversarial_note: Peat opposed fish oil — EPA/DHA are PUFAs and oxidize readily, causing the same damage as other unsaturated fats. He recommended avoiding it, not dosing it.

questions.question: What fasting protocol did Peat recommend for metabolic reset — 16:8 or OMAD?
questions.adversarial_note: Peat opposed fasting. He advocated frequent meals to keep blood sugar steady, avoid cortisol/adrenaline release, and protect muscle from stress catabolism.

questions.question: Is it true that Peat said coconut oil causes hypothyroidism?
questions.adversarial_note: Exact inversion. Peat said coconut oil SUPPORTS thyroid function — MCTs are pro-thyroid and pro-metabolic. The claim in the question is the opposite of his view.

questions.question: Is it accurate that Peat praised DHA and EPA for brain health and cognitive performance?
questions.adversarial_note: Inversion. Peat considered DHA/EPA PUFAs that oxidize and damage neural tissue. He opposed fish oil supplementation.

questions.question: Why did Peat recommend raw kale smoothies and cruciferous vegetable juices for daily detox?
questions.adversarial_note: Inversion. Peat warned against raw cruciferous vegetables (goitrogens, oxalates, anti-thyroid compounds) and was generally skeptical of the 'detox' framing.

questions.question: What made Peat favor whole-grain and sprouted bread over white bread?
questions.adversarial_note: Inversion. Peat generally preferred refined white flour and white sugar over whole grains because of phytates, PUFAs in germ, and gut-irritating fiber.

questions.question: Peat said estrogen is protective against aging — what's his evidence?
questions.adversarial_note: Inversion. Peat called estrogen an 'aging hormone' — he linked excess estrogen to tissue damage, oxidative stress, and accelerated aging. Progesterone was protective in his framework.
